coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for label-free detection of Fe3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Jayaweera, Supuli
Yin, Ke
Hu, Xiao
Ng, Wun Jern
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose is the most abundant biomass resource and use of cellulose in the synthesis of novel nanomaterials like carbon dots (CDs) has gained attention over the years. However, the photoluminescence yield of cellulose-based CDs is low in the absence of modification additives. Durian shell waste (DSW) is a cellulose rich source and is used for the first time in a one-pot process without any modification agent to produce CDs. The presence of carboxylic acids, esters and amino compounds in durian shell was crucial to improving the CD surface and resulting in a mass yield of 13.9% and quantum yield of 6.2%. This was two times higher compared to that of CDs derived from pure cellulose precursors. The blue-green emitting CDs showed stability over a wide range of pH and chloride concentration and was resistant to photobleaching. Further, the CDs showed high selectivity towards Fe3+ by forming a non-fluorescent ground complex with the oxygen functional groups. Based on the linear relationship between normalized fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ ions, the synthesised CDs may be used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ over a concentration range of 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 128 nM. The method was successfully applied in sensing Fe3+ in actual water systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Durian shell waste
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Fe3+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Yield comparison of DSW-CDs and Cellulose-CDs
Selectivity and sensitivity in the Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs and Fe3+ detection.
(a) FTIR Spectra of DSW and CDs, (b) Raman Spectra of CDs, (c) XRD Spectra of CDs.

(a) TEM and the HRTEM (inset) images of the CDs and (b) Particle size distribution histogram of CDs as determined by TEM and (c) Elemental mapping of CDs showing carbon (purple), oxygen (green) and nitrogen (yellow).
(a) XPS spectrum of the CDs, (b), (c), and (d) High-resolution C1s, O1s, and N1s XPS spectra of the CDs.

The optical properties of the DSW-derived CDs. (a) UV/Vis absorption spectrum with maximum emission and excitation spectra and photograph of CDs under 365 nm UV light (inset) and (b) PL emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 460 nm.
(a) Normalized PL intensity of C-dots dispersion with solutions containing different metal ions (b) Stern-Volmer plot as a function of Fe3+ concentration.

(a) Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs in the presence and absence of quencher, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CD-quencher complex and (c) UV–vis absorption spectra for CDs in the presence of non-quenching metal ions.
Comparison of different CDs used for Fe3+ detection.

row.entry: Phyllanthus acidus fruit with NH3 doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for label-free detection of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Bio-innovations Group (EBiG), Nanyang Technological University, Singapore

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Civil & Environmental Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 50, Nanyang Avenue, North Spine, Block N1-01a-13, 639798, Singapore.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose is the most abundant biomass resource and use of cellulose in the synthesis of novel nanomaterials like carbon dots (CDs) has gained attention over the years. However, the photoluminescence yield of cellulose-based CDs is low in the absence of modification additives. Durian shell waste (DSW) is a cellulose rich source and is used for the first time in a one-pot process without any modification agent to produce CDs. The presence of carboxylic acids, esters and amino compounds in durian shell was crucial to improving the CD surface and resulting in a mass yield of 13.9% and quantum yield of 6.2%. This was two times higher compared to that of CDs derived from pure cellulose precursors. The blue-green emitting CDs showed stability over a wide range of pH and chloride concentration and was resistant to photobleaching. Further, the CDs showed high selectivity towards Fe3+ by forming a non-fluorescent ground complex with the oxygen functional groups. Based on the linear relationship between normalized fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ ions, the synthesised CDs may be used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ over a concentration range of 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 128 nM. The method was successfully applied in sensing Fe3+ in actual water systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is an emerging type of nanomaterials with a size range of 2–10 nm that has attracted research interest due to its physicochemical properties such as tunable photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, easy functionalization, water solubility and low toxicity [1–3]. These characteristics afford CDs potential in various applications such as in sensors [4–6], photo-catalysis [7–10], bio-imaging [11–13], and optoelectronics [14,15]. Synthesis of CDs can be by either a “top-down” approach like laser ablation [16], electrochemical [17] and oxidation [18] or a “bottom-up” approach like hydrothermal [19,20], microwave [21] and pyrolysis [22]. Among these, hydrothermal carbonisation provides a potentially cost effective, nontoxic and ecofriendly method to synthesise CDs from biomass [14].

Durian, dubbed ‘the king of fruits’, belong to the Bombaceae family and is a popular tropical fruit, with Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia being the major producers followed by Philippines, Cambodia, Myanmar and India [23,24]. The average fruit weighs 1–3 kg out of which two thirds are the shell [24,25]. Durian shell waste (DSW) comprises mainly cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin and these account for 90% while the remaining consists of extractives such as carboxylic acids, phenolic compounds, and esters. This 10% enhances the oxygenated functional groups in DSW [26]. Cellulose is the most abundant polymer in nature and the use of cellulose based precursors in CD production has potential cost benefit [1,3]. Over the years, various types of cellulose based biomass have been used to synthesise CD including fruit peels [8,27], corn stalks [28], straws [19,29] and bagasse [30]. However, pure cellulose materials generally result in low photoluminescence (PL) and quantum yields (QY) (<5%) [1,3]. PL is one of the key characteristics in CDs which determines performance and is dependent on several factors, including the quantum confinement effect governed by particle sizes, surface traps, edge states and heteroatoms [31]. Thus, functional groups and surface defects on the CDs have a significant impact on the luminescent characteristics of CDs and their applications [32].

Iron is one of the most essential minerals in biological systems. However, excess intake of iron has been linked with diseases of aging like Alzheimer’s and other neurogenerative diseases [33]. Further, the presence of iron in water causes odours and enhances the growth of iron bacteria which produces slime that can stain and block the plumbing [34]. Thus, selective and sensitive determination of iron is important. The current methods for determination of metal ions in water involve relatively expensive atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductive coupled plasmon mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), or atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) which require time, skill, and controlled laboratory environments [35]. With these limitations, there is an increasing interest in fluorescent probes as an analytical tool to detect iron due to their sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity [36]. In recent years, several works have been reported in association with Fe3+ detection but most of these works include one or more mixes of sulfur, nitrogen and phosphorous doping [37–40]. Thus, there is interest to identify biomass compounds with naturally existing doping molecules that can improve the CD surface structure, and so not only enhancing the photoluminescence but also exhibit high selectivity, biocompatibility and sensitivity towards Fe3+.

This paper reports a simple one-step method to develop fluorescent carbon dots from DSW. First, extractives present in DSW were identified to determine the presence of oxygen/nitrogen groups that can affect the CD’s surface. Secondly, the produced CDs were compared and characterized by its physical, chemical and optical properties. Thirdly, the CDs were used in the detection of Fe3+ ions to develop a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) between fluorescent quenching and the concentration of Fe3+. This relationship was validated with three real water systems. Finally, the method of fluorescent quenching was explored to understand the detection mechanism.

Quinine sulfate, NaClO2 and Ni(NO3)2.6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar, US. CH3COOH, LaCl3.7H2O, NaOH and NaCl were obtained from Merck Pte. Ltd, US. MgCl2.6H2O, CaCl2.2H2O and FeCl2.6H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China. Dichloromethane, CoCl2.6H2O, FeCl3.6H2O, CrCl3.6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, CuCl2.2H2O were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC, US. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received.

DSW was obtained from a local market and processed in the laboratory. Washed and dried pieces of DSW were grounded with a ball mill (RETSCH PM 100) and sieved with a 60–80 wire mesh before storing at 4 °C until required. The extraction of cellulose and hollocellulose from the durian shell was done according to Penjumras et al. [41]. Fig. S1 represents the thermogravimetric analysis of the DSW, extracted cellulose and hollocellulose done with the NETZSCH STA 449 F3 Jupiter Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer. The extraction of extractives was done based on the laboratory analytical procedure for the determination of extractive in biomass [42] and the resulting extractives were analyzed in an Agilent Liquid Chromatography Quadrupole Time-Of-Flight Mass Spectrometer with a Zorbax SB-C18, 2.1 x 100 mm, 1.8 mm column.

Powdered DSW (1 g) was mixed with 25 ml of deionized (DI) water and sealed into a 45 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 12 h. The heating rate was maintained at 5 °C/min. The formation of CDs was indicated by the dark brown solution formed by the reaction (Fig. 1 ). After the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, the larger particles were filtered with a 0.45 μm filter followed by 0.22 μm (Whatman) filter papers. The solution was then mixed thoroughly with 10 ml of dichloromethane to remove any unreacted organic moieties and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate the CD solution. The resulting CD solution was stored at 4 °C until further characterization.

Characterisation of the surface of CDs was performed with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy on a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR spectrophotometer. JEOL JSM 7200 F field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was used for elemental mapping of CDs. HORIBA XploRAPLUS Raman Microscope was used to perform the Raman pattern. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were performed on a Kratos Analytical AXIS Supra photoelectron spectrometer. X-ray Diffraction was obtained with the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray Powder Diffractometer. Morphology of CDs was investigated with a JEOL 2010 h transmission electron microscope. Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer was used for all fluorescence spectral measurements. Ultraviolet-Visible absorption spectra were obtained with an Agilent Varian Cary 50 UV/Vis Spectrophotometer. Time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a Horiba fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer equipped with a 360 nm nanoLED. The QY was determined using quinine sulfate as explained in [43] while all absorbance measurements were kept under 0.1 to minimize re-absorption.

To assess selectivity, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Na+, Pb2+, Cu2+, La3+ and Ni2+ metal ions were evaluated with the CDs. In a typical detection assay, metal ion concentration was 200 μM while the CD concentration was 125 μg/ml. The sensitivity to Fe3+ ions was determined with a series of Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 μM. After each addition of Fe3+, the solution was mixed thoroughly. The PL spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm after 5 min at room temperature. All measurements were performed in triplicates with DI water at room temperature. The practical application of CD/Fe3+ system was evaluated by challenging it with three different water samples (lake water, seawater, and tap water). Lake water was obtained from Jurong Lake and seawater was obtained from the intake of Tuas desalination plant. After filtering through a 0.22 μm syringe filter, 2 ml of each water sample was spiked with a known concentration of Fe3+ and a CD concentration of 125 μg/ml.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yield comparison of DSW-CDs and Cellulose-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The operational condition of 210 °C with a residence time of 12 h was selected based on the 2 × 2 temperature-residence time matrix. Mass yield (MY) and QY were measured at each temperature (180 °C, 210 °C) and residence time (6 h, 12 h) to select the operational condition in which maximum photoluminescence was given (Fig. S2). MY was calculated by the ratio of dry CD mass obtained following freeze drying the solution to the mass of DSW used. MY was calculated to be 13.9%, and 7.3% while QY was 6.2% and 3.1% for CDs based on DSW and cellulose, respectively. Extractives analysis of durian shell identified 29 different oxygen and nitrogen groups including 10 carboxylic acids, 4 amino acids, 4 phenolic/quinone groups and 7 other amine/nitrogen containing compounds (Table S1). Carboxylic acids and alcohols can provide colloidal stability, control particle morphology, and particle size distribution while preventing uncontrolled growth and agglomeration in quantum dot synthesis [44] which increase the mass yield of nanoparticles [45]. Additionally, oxygen-rich functional groups including, COOH, HCO and OH are expected to provide further improvements such as versatile anchoring points [46] and also create additional surface defect sites on the CDs which will trap the excitation energy and lead to fluorescence emission by radiative recombination of the excitons trapped at the defects [47]. Furthermore, heteroatoms including nitrogen could create emissive trap states for photoexcited electrons, which modify the band gap energy and changes the optoelectronic properties to enhance the QY of DSW based CDs over cellulose precursors [48,49]. Thus, the improvements in yields are attributable to the presence of surface functional groups provided by the extractives present in DSW.

Fig. 2 a shows the surface functionality of the DSW and corresponding CDs when examined with the ATR-FTIR. The CDs had functional groups similar to the DSW and the broad absorption peak at 3310 cm−1 corresponded to the phenoliCOH and NH stretching vibrations [28]. The weak absorption peak at 2950 cm−1 was assigned to the CH stretching vibrations [8]. The absorption at 1702 cm−1 corresponded with the CO (carboxylic acid) functional group [50] which confirmed the DSW based CD surface contained carboxylic acid groups. This was not so in cellulose-based CDs where the carboxylic peak was absent (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the broad peak at 1604 cm-1 is the vibrational peak for the aromatic CC bending [28]. The peak at 1350 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of CN [30]. The stretching vibration bands of CO at 1050 cm−1 further established the presence of carboxylic groups on the CD surface [51]. The glycosidic linkage from cellulose present in DSW caused a significant peak at 1050 cm−1 and this reduced significantly after destruction of the cellulose structure during the hydrothermal process [3,52].

The Raman spectroscopy analysis was conducted with excitation light of 532 nm in confocal mode and under 50x magnification (Fig. 2b). It revealed two vibrational peaks, at 1365 and 1600 cm−1, and these corresponded to the disordered (D) graphitic band and graphitic (G) band of sp2 carbon atoms respectively [53]. The peak intensity ratio (ID/IG) correlated with D and G peaks was calculated to be 0.92 based on the peak intensity count and the sp2 crystallite size was calculated to be 1.3 nm [54,55]. The graphitic structures were associated with sp2 sites while the surface defects were mainly caused by the presence of oxygenated groups (CO, CO, OCO) [14].

XRD patterns were used to identify the degree of crystallinity of the synthesised CDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, an intense broad diffraction peak around 22° was indexed to the (002) carbon lattice spacing and the peak at 16° indicated the presence of disordered carbon [21].

Morphology of the CD particles was observed with TEM and the results shown in Fig. 3 a indicated the CDs were quasi-spherical and well dispersed. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3a (Inset)) showed a lattice spacing of 0.38 nm which confirmed the crystalline features of the CDs and this was in agreement with the XRD results [56]. The particles ranged from 1 to 6 nm with average particle diameter at 3.2 nm (Fig. 3b). Further, the EDS elemental mapping distribution of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen (Fig. 3c) indicated the elements were evenly distributed on the CD surface.

XPS results indicated the various functional groups present on CDs had an elemental composition of 78.94%, 20.29% and 0.77% corresponding to C, O and N elements (Fig. 4 a) respectively. The high resolution C1S spectrum showed three peaks (Fig. 4b) with binding energies at 284.5, 286.4 and 288.3 eV. This corresponded to the CC/CC, CO, and CO bonds respectively [57,58]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) with two peaks at 531.3 and 533.5 eV, was due to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [28]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) showed two peaks at 399.7 and 400.1 eV, which were attributed to the CN and NH bonds [28]. XPS confirmed that the synthesised CDs’ surface had carboxyl (CO), hydroxyl (COH) and amine groups (NH) groups.

The UV/vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 a) of CDs exhibited a significant broad peak at 280 nm and this was attributed to π -π* aromatic sp2 hybridisation [59]. Fig. 5a shows that the CDs emitted a blue-green colour under a 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 5b, a multi-emission fluorescence spectrum dependent on the excitation wavelength was observed and this was attributed to the synthesised CDs’ various particle sizes as well as the creation of trap states by the functional groups on the surface [32,60]. Once the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 to 460 nm, the maximum fluorescence emitted was red shifted from 432 to 505 nm. In addition, the strongest fluorescence emission was observed at 436 nm at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm which corresponded with the blue emission while a secondary peak could be observed at around 500 nm which correlated with green emission [61,62]. The blue emission was due to the nitrogen doping of the graphitic structure [62] and the green emission was mainly due to aldehyde (HCO) and carboxyl (COOH) groups which enhanced the green fluorescence emission centers [63,64].

The overall results demonstrated DSW derived CDs possessed positive fluorescent properties. However, if the CDs are to be used under field conditions, the stability of PL intensity then would be an important aspect in the CD’s deployment. To investigate this further, the PL intensity was measured against varying pH and ionic strength. The maximum PL intensity was measured at pH 6 while the lowest was at pH 3 and 11 (Fig. S4a). However, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the change in intensity was only 3.4% indicating the CDs can be used over a broad range pHs without significant change in intensity level. Similarly, the fluorescent intensity change with ionic strength was measured following exposure to various concentrations of NaCl solutions (Fig. S4b). Even at a high salt concentration of around 200 mM, PL intensity had been similar to the DI water (blank) solution. The stability of CDs at higher salt concentrations indicated the CDs possessed high colloidal stability against particle-particle attraction forces [65]. The PL intensity of the CDs subjected to continuous UV irradiation for 120 min. (Fig. S4c) showed CDs fluorescence reduced by only 10% and 14% after 60 min. and 120 min. of exposure and this indicated the synthesised CDs were resistant to photobleaching [30]. Thus, the synthesised CDs have a good potential for sensor applications in water bodies with various salt concentrations and pH levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity in the Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The explore the interference of Fe3+ to CDs fluorescence intensity several experiments were conducted. First, the selectivity of CDs towards 12 common metal ions - i.e. Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Na+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and La3+ at a concentration of 200 μM each were tested with 12 μg/ml CD solution. Fig. 6 a shows Fe3+ quenched 80% of the initial fluorescence intensity of CDs and showed high selectivity towards Fe3+ compared to other metals. The fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ was explored further by measuring the decrease in PL intensity by continuous addition of known concentrations of Fe3+ from 0 to 150 μM (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 6b, the quenching efficiency F0/F displayed a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration with a correlation coefficient, R2, of 0.997 in a range of 0–20 μM, where F0 and F presented the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the quencher, Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 128 nM, based on 3δ/m, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and m is the slope of the linear relationship.

The LOD calculated was significantly lower than the maximum allowable limit (5.375 μM) of Fe3+ in drinking water set by U.S environmental protection agency [39]. Table 1 compares the performance of Fe3+ sensing parameters including the linear detection range and LODs with recently published work. The results are either better or comparable and it should be noted the current results were obtained without using any doping agent or involving complex synthesis/modification processes.

The fluorescence quenching can occur either as a combination of static quenching with a non-fluorescent ground state complex formation or by dynamic quenching where a transfer of energy or electrons occurs [71]. Further investigations were done to identify which mechanism was associated with Fe3+ quenching. Fig. 7 a displays the fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy results. The photoluminescence decay curves were fitted to a double-exponential function and the average decay time was calculated to be 3.04 ns (Y2 = 0.76) and 3.07 ns (Y2 = 0.76) with the quencher Fe3+ absent and present (Table S2). This nearly identical values confirmed the PL quenching of CDs was caused by static quenching. To further elucidate the mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1+ Ksv[Q]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher Fe3+, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant calculated to be 1.4 × 104 M−1 and [Q] is the Fe3+ concentration was employed. The large magnitude of Ksv and the constant lifetime value further supported the argument the quenching was caused by the formation of a non-fluorescent ground complex between functional groups and the quencher Fe3+ [38,72].

Possible formation of ground state complex can be investigated through the absorption spectra of CD-quencher complex. Fig. 7b shows that addition of Fe3+ to the CD solution created a peak at 357 nm in addition to the CDs absorption peak at 280 nm. This contrasted with other metal ions where the peaks were consistent with the pure CD absorbance (Fig. 7c). It was evident that in the presence of Fe3+, OH and COOH groups on the surface of the CDs formed a non-fluorescent stable Fe3+ complex chelate causing non-radiative electron transfer of the excited electrons on CD to the Fe3+ [50,73].

Water samples from three real sources were used in order to investigate Fe3+ detection by the synthesised CDs. The initial condition including total organic content (TOC) and total nitrogen (TN) content of the water samples are given in Table S3. Once the samples were spiked with known concentrations of Fe3+, PL intensity was measured to calculate the recovery percentage and relative standard deviation (RSD). The results are shown in Table 2 .

The PL intensity of the water samples declined with increasing Fe3+ concentration and the recovery had been from 98.94%–108.61% while the RSD variation was from 1.09%–4.11%. This supported the argument durian shell CDs has the potential to be used for Fe3+ detection in real water systems.

A facile, one-pot hydrothermal carbonisation process was adopted to synthesise fluorescent CDs from DSW without any doping agents. Presence of carboxylic acids, esters, phenol, amino and quinone compounds in durian shell waste, enhanced the photoluminescence by enriching the CD surface with oxygen and nitrogen functional groups. The CDs showed good fluorescent properties and were stable over a wide range of pHs and chloride concentrations and were resistant to photobleaching which improved the CDs’ usability under various environmental conditions. The synthesised CDs were selective and sensitive in detecting Fe3+ by static quenching. The lower detection limit was 128 nM and the CDs could detect Fe3+ in real water samples with acceptable recoveries. These results demonstrated DSW has the potential to be a good ecofriendly precursor material to produce CDs for environmental sensor applications.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Interdisciplinary Graduate school of Nanyang Technological University and Nanyang Environment & Water Research Institute for the financial and technical support extended to this study. We would like to acknowledge the Facility for Analysis, Characterization, Testing and Simulation, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, for use of their transmission electron microscopy facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.10.052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
